topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too bright, a harsh, unforgiving glare washing over the canvases. Elara stood near the entrance, her breath catching in her throat with every measured step toward the center of the room. Each brushstroke, each vibrant splash of color she had poured her soul into, was now on display, framed and polished under spotlights. A wave of icy apprehension washed over her, tightening her chest until drawing a full breath became a conscious, difficult effort. She watched a stranger approach a painting, their head tilted in contemplation, and a sudden, sharp instinct urged her to retreat, to vanish into the shadows of the hallway. Her hands clenched into tight fists at her sides, the fabric of her dress suddenly feeling like a suffocating restraint.

[story 2]

"This is… not right," Marcus whispered, his voice barely audible above the hushed murmur of the crowd. He circled the installation slowly, his gaze lingering on the unfinished sculpture that was undeniably his. A tremor ran through his hands, making him grip the edge of a nearby pedestal until his knuckles were white. He took a step back, his eyes darting from the artwork to the security guard stationed near the exit, searching for any sign of authority, any indication that this intrusion could be stopped. A coldness settled deep in his core, a heavy weight pressing down on his shoulders, making him want to simply dissolve into the polished floor.

[story 3]

I couldn't look away from the way the light caught the texture of my latest piece. It was stunning, undeniably so, but the realization that these images, my private language, were now being consumed by strangers made my stomach clench violently. My palms began to sweat, slicking my fingertips as I tried to maintain a facade of composure. Every footstep echoed unnervingly in the vast, silent space. I pressed my back against a cold marble pillar, focusing intensely on the intricate pattern of the stone, hoping that if I just stayed still enough, the entire overwhelming situation would somehow pass over me, unnoticed.

Elara stood near the entrance, her breath catching in her throat with every measured step toward the center of the room. Each brushstroke, each vibrant splash of color she had poured her soul into, was now on display, framed and polished under spotlights. A wave of icy apprehension washed over her, tightening her chest until drawing a full breath became a conscious, difficult effort. She watched a stranger approach a painting, their head tilted in contemplation, and a sudden, sharp instinct urged her to retreat, to vanish into the shadows of the hallway. Her hands clenched into tight fists at her sides, the fabric of her dress suddenly feeling like a suffocating restraint.

"This is… not right," Marcus whispered, his voice barely audible above the hushed murmur of the crowd. He circled the installation slowly, his gaze lingering on the unfinished sculpture that was undeniably his. A tremor ran through his hands, making him grip the edge of a nearby pedestal until his knuckles were white. He took a step back, his eyes darting from the artwork to the security guard stationed near the exit, searching for any sign of authority, any indication that this intrusion could be stopped. A coldness settled deep in his core, a heavy weight pressing down on his shoulders, making him want to simply dissolve into the polished floor.

I couldn't look away from the way the light caught the texture of my latest piece. It was stunning, undeniably so, but the realization that these images, my private language, were now being consumed by strangers made my stomach clench violently. My palms began to sweat, slicking my fingertips as I tried to maintain a facade of composure. Every footstep echoed unnervingly in the vast, silent space. I pressed my back against a cold marble pillar, focusing intensely on the intricate pattern of the stone, hoping that if I just stayed still enough, the entire overwhelming situation would somehow pass over me, unnoticed.
